```{r 09_ml_pointwise_elpd}
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Pointwise ELPD: ML-WBA vs. ML-SBA",
    subtitle = "Faceted by agent type. Advantage grows with evidence conflict for non-balanced agents.",
    x = "Evidence Conflict |prop_direct − prop_social|",
    y = "ELPD Difference (ML-WBA − ML-SBA)",
    colour = "Agent type"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The expected pattern: for balanced agents ($\rho \approx 0.5$), the ML-WBA offers little advantage over the ML-SBA — the extra parameters are wasted. For agents who deviate substantially from balanced weighting, the ML-WBA should gain a progressively larger advantage on high-conflict trials, where the sources disagree and the weighting asymmetry determines the response.

## Model Recovery: Multilevel Level

Model recovery at the multilevel level asks: *if data are generated by a specific multilevel model, can our comparison pipeline correctly identify it?* We test three scenarios, paralleling the single-agent analysis.

### Specific Scenarios

Before the full simulation, we examine three instructive scenarios:

1. **SBA-Mimic Population:** All agents have $\rho = 0.5, \kappa = 2.0$ (i.e., $w_d = w_s = 1$) with zero individual variation. The population *is* the SBA.

2. **PBA Population:** Agents vary in $\rho$ (drawn from a population) but all have $\kappa = 1.0$. The total evidence budget is fixed; only allocation varies.

3. **Full WBA Population:** Agents vary in both $\rho$ and $\kappa$. The full hierarchical model is needed.

```{r 09_ml_scenario_generation}
set.seed(2026)

# Helper: generate multilevel data from a parameter specification
generate_ml_data <- function(agent_rhos, agent_kappas, ev, n_reps = 2) {
  J <- length(agent_rhos)
  all_data <- map_dfr(seq_len(J), function(j) {
    wd <- agent_rhos[j] * agent_kappas[j]
    ws <- (1 - agent_rhos[j]) * agent_kappas[j]
    generate_agent_decisions(wd, ws, ev, n_samples = n_reps) |>
      mutate(agent_id = j, total1 = 8L, total2 = 3L)
  })
  list(
    N = nrow(all_data), J = J,
    agent_id = all_data$agent_id,
    choice   = all_data$choice,
    blue1    = all_data$blue1,
    total1   = all_data$total1,
    blue2    = all_data$blue2,
    total2   = all_data$total2
  )
}

J_scen <- 15

# Scenario 1: SBA-Mimic (no individual variation)
scen1_data <- generate_ml_data(
  agent_rhos   = rep(0.5, J_scen),
  agent_kappas = rep(2.0, J_scen),
  ev = evidence_combinations
)

# Scenario 2: PBA Population (rho varies, kappa = 1)
scen2_rhos <- plogis(rnorm(J_scen, 0.5, 0.5))
scen2_data <- generate_ml_data(
  agent_rhos   = scen2_rhos,
  agent_kappas = rep(1.0, J_scen),
  ev = evidence_combinations
)

# Scenario 3: Full WBA population (both vary)
scen3_draws <- MASS::mvrnorm(J_scen, mu = c(0.3, log(2.5)),
                              Sigma = matrix(c(0.5, -0.15, -0.15, 0.2), 2))
scen3_data <- generate_ml_data(
  agent_rhos   = plogis(scen3_draws[, 1]),
  agent_kappas = exp(scen3_draws[, 2]),
  ev = evidence_combinations
)
```

```{r 09_ml_scenario_fits, message=FALSE}
fit_ml_scenario <- function(stan_data, label) {
  cat(sprintf("\n--- Fitting scenario: %s ---\n", label))

  sba <- mod_ml_sba$sample(
    data = stan_data, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000, adapt_delta = 0.9,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  pba <- mod_ml_pba$sample(
    data = stan_data, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )
  wba <- mod_ml_wba$sample(
    data = stan_data, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 1000,
    iter_sampling = 1000, adapt_delta = 0.95,
    refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
  )

  # Return pre-computed LOO objects, not the raw fits
  list(
    SBA = loo(sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix")),
    PBA = loo(pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix")),
    WBA = loo(wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
  )
}

if (regenerate_simulations) {
  loos_scen1 <- fit_ml_scenario(scen1_data, "SBA-Mimic Population")
  loos_scen2 <- fit_ml_scenario(scen2_data, "PBA Population")
  loos_scen3 <- fit_ml_scenario(scen3_data, "Full WBA Population")
  saveRDS(list(s1 = loos_scen1, s2 = loos_scen2, s3 = loos_scen3),
          "simmodels/ch9_ml_scenario_loos.rds")
} else {
  loos_all   <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_ml_scenario_loos.rds")
  loos_scen1 <- loos_all$s1
  loos_scen2 <- loos_all$s2
  loos_scen3 <- loos_all$s3
}
```

```{r 09_ml_scenario_comparison}
compute_ml_scenario <- function(loos, scenario_name) {
  cat(sprintf("\n=== %s ===\n", scenario_name))
  comp <- loo_compare(loos)
  print(comp)

  w <- loo_model_weights(loos, method = "stacking")
  cat("Stacking weights:\n")
  print(round(w, 3))

  list(loos = loos, weights = w, comparison = comp)
}

res_scen1 <- compute_ml_scenario(loos_scen1, "Scenario 1: SBA-Mimic Population")
res_scen2 <- compute_ml_scenario(loos_scen2, "Scenario 2: PBA Population")
res_scen3 <- compute_ml_scenario(loos_scen3, "Scenario 3: Full WBA Population")

# Build summary table
extract_ml_loo <- function(res, name) {
  comp <- as_tibble(res$comparison, rownames = "Model") |>
    select(Model, elpd_diff, se_diff)
  w <- tibble(Model = names(res$weights),
              weight = as.numeric(res$weights))
  comp |> left_join(w, by = "Model") |> mutate(Scenario = name)
}

ml_scenario_summary <- bind_rows(
  extract_ml_loo(res_scen1, "1. SBA-Mimic"),
  extract_ml_loo(res_scen2, "2. PBA Population"),
  extract_ml_loo(res_scen3, "3. Full WBA")
) |>
  mutate(
    Model    = factor(Model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")),
    Scenario = factor(Scenario)
  )

# ELPD differences
p_ml_elpd <- ggplot(ml_scenario_summary,
                     aes(x = Model, y = elpd_diff, colour = Model)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = elpd_diff - se_diff,
                       ymax = elpd_diff + se_diff),
                  size = 0.8, linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ Scenario) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Multilevel ELPD Differences", y = "ELPD Difference", x = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11))

# Stacking weights
p_ml_weight <- ggplot(ml_scenario_summary,
                       aes(x = Model, y = weight, fill = Model)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(weight, accuracy = 1)),
            vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ Scenario) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1.2)) +
  labs(title = "Multilevel Stacking Weights", y = "Stacking Weight", x = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "none", strip.text = element_blank())

p_ml_elpd / p_ml_weight +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Multilevel Model Recovery: Scenario Case Studies",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"))
  )
```

The expected pattern mirrors the single-agent case. In Scenario 1, the SBA-mimic population has no individual variation in weights, so the ML-SBA (zero parameters) should win or tie with the ML-PBA and ML-WBA — complexity is penalised. In Scenario 2, the PBA population requires individual variation in $\rho$ but not in $\kappa$, so the ML-PBA should win. In Scenario 3, the full WBA population needs both $\rho$ and $\kappa$ variation, and the ML-WBA should dominate.

### LOO Pointwise Diagnostics Across Scenarios

```{r 09_ml_scenario_pointwise, fig.width=10, fig.height=5}
make_ml_pw <- function(loos, stan_data, scenario_name, ref_model = "SBA") {
  elpd_wba <- loos$WBA$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"]
  elpd_ref <- loos[[ref_model]]$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"]
  
  tibble(
    obs       = seq_len(stan_data$N),
    blue1     = stan_data$blue1,
    blue2     = stan_data$blue2,
    elpd_diff = elpd_wba - elpd_ref,
    Scenario  = scenario_name
  ) |>
    mutate(
      evidence_conflict = abs(blue1 / 8 - blue2 / 3)
    )
}

ml_pw <- bind_rows(
  make_ml_pw(res_scen1$loos, scen1_data,
             "1. SBA-Mimic\n(ML-WBA vs ML-SBA)", "SBA"),
  make_ml_pw(res_scen2$loos, scen2_data,
             "2. PBA Population\n(ML-WBA vs ML-PBA)", "PBA"),
  make_ml_pw(res_scen3$loos, scen3_data,
             "3. Full WBA\n(ML-WBA vs ML-PBA)", "PBA")
)

ggplot(ml_pw, aes(x = evidence_conflict, y = elpd_diff)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_point(aes(colour = elpd_diff > 0), size = 1.5, alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "gam", formula = y ~ s(x, bs = "cs"),
              colour = "midnightblue", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ Scenario) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "firebrick", "TRUE" = "steelblue"),
    labels = c("FALSE" = "Favours simpler model", "TRUE" = "Favours ML-WBA"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Pointwise ELPD: Where Does Complexity Help?",
    subtitle = "In mimicking scenarios (left), the WBA loses by a constant complexity penalty.\nIn the full WBA population (right), advantage emerges on high-conflict trials.",
    x = "Evidence Conflict |prop_direct − prop_social|",
    y = "ELPD Difference"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

### Full Model Recovery Simulation

We now scale up to a full model recovery simulation at the multilevel level. For each of the three generating models (ML-SBA, ML-PBA, ML-WBA), we generate multiple synthetic populations, cross-fit all three candidates, and examine the confusion matrix and calibration.

```{r 09_ml_model_recovery_setup}
n_ml_sims <- 30  # Per generating model
J_recovery <- 12  # Agents per simulated population

if (regenerate_simulations) {
  
  ml_recovery_results <- list()
  counter <- 0
  
  for (true_model in c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")) {
    for (sim_id in seq_len(n_ml_sims)) {
      counter <- counter + 1
      
      # Generate population parameters
      if (true_model == "SBA") {
        rhos   <- rep(0.5, J_recovery)
        kappas <- rep(2.0, J_recovery)
      } else if (true_model == "PBA") {
        rhos   <- plogis(rnorm(J_recovery, 0, 0.6))
        kappas <- rep(1.0, J_recovery)
      } else {
        draws_raw <- MASS::mvrnorm(
          J_recovery,
          mu    = c(0, log(2)),
          Sigma = matrix(c(0.4, -0.1, -0.1, 0.15), 2)
        )
        rhos   <- plogis(draws_raw[, 1])
        kappas <- exp(draws_raw[, 2])
      }
      
      sim_stan <- generate_ml_data(rhos, kappas, evidence_combinations, n_reps = 2)
      
      # Fit all three models (reduced iterations for speed)
      f_sba <- mod_ml_sba$sample(
        data = sim_stan, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500,
        iter_sampling = 500, adapt_delta = 0.9,
        refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
      )
      f_pba <- mod_ml_pba$sample(
        data = sim_stan, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500,
        iter_sampling = 500, adapt_delta = 0.95,
        refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
      )
      f_wba <- mod_ml_wba$sample(
        data = sim_stan, chains = 2, iter_warmup = 500,
        iter_sampling = 500, adapt_delta = 0.95,
        refresh = 0, show_messages = FALSE
      )
      
      # Compute LOO and stacking weights
      l_sba <- loo(f_sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
      l_pba <- loo(f_pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
      l_wba <- loo(f_wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
      
      w <- tryCatch(
        loo_model_weights(
          list(SBA = l_sba, PBA = l_pba, WBA = l_wba),
          method = "stacking"
        ),
        error = function(e) c(SBA = 1/3, PBA = 1/3, WBA = 1/3)
      )
      
      ml_recovery_results[[counter]] <- tibble(
        sim_id     = sim_id,
        true_model = true_model,
        weight_SBA = as.numeric(w["SBA"]),
        weight_PBA = as.numeric(w["PBA"]),
        weight_WBA = as.numeric(w["WBA"]),
        mean_true_rho   = mean(rhos),
        sd_true_rho     = sd(rhos),
        mean_true_kappa = mean(kappas),
        sd_true_kappa   = sd(kappas)
      )
      
      if (counter %% 10 == 0) {
        cat(sprintf("  Completed %d / %d simulations\n", counter, 3 * n_ml_sims))
      }
    }
  }
  
  ml_recovery_df <- bind_rows(ml_recovery_results)
  saveRDS(ml_recovery_df, "simmodels/ch9_ml_model_recovery.rds")
  
} else {
  ml_recovery_df <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_ml_model_recovery.rds")
}
```

### Confusion Matrix

```{r 09_ml_confusion_matrix}
# Binary confusion matrix (max-weight winner)
ml_binary <- ml_recovery_df |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("weight_"),
               names_to = "predicted_model", values_to = "weight") |>
  mutate(predicted_model = str_remove(predicted_model, "weight_")) |>
  group_by(sim_id, true_model) |>
  slice_max(weight, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  count(true_model, predicted_model) |>
  group_by(true_model) |>
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    true_model      = factor(true_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")),
    predicted_model = factor(predicted_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA"))
  ) |>
  complete(true_model, predicted_model, fill = list(n = 0, proportion = 0))

ml_binary |>
  select(-n) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = predicted_model, values_from = proportion) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
               caption = "Multilevel Binary Confusion Matrix: Win Rate")

ml_binary |>
  ggplot(aes(x = predicted_model, y = true_model, fill = proportion)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(proportion, accuracy = 1)),
            size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "mako", direction = -1, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Model Recovery: Binary Classification",
    x = "Winning Candidate Model",
    y = "True Generating Model",
    fill = "Win Rate"
  ) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())
```

```{r 09_ml_probabilistic_confusion}
# Probabilistic confusion matrix (average stacking weights)
ml_prob_confusion <- ml_recovery_df |>
  group_by(true_model) |>
  summarise(
    Recovered_SBA = mean(weight_SBA),
    Recovered_PBA = mean(weight_PBA),
    Recovered_WBA = mean(weight_WBA),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(true_model = factor(true_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA")))

knitr::kable(ml_prob_confusion, digits = 2,
             caption = "Multilevel Probabilistic Confusion Matrix: Mean Stacking Weights")

ml_prob_confusion |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("Recovered"),
               names_to = "fitted_model", values_to = "weight") |>
  mutate(fitted_model = factor(str_remove(fitted_model, "Recovered_"),
                                levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fitted_model, y = true_model, fill = weight)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(weight, accuracy = 1)),
            size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "mako", direction = -1, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Model Recovery: Average Stacking Weights",
    x = "Candidate Model",
    y = "True Generating Model",
    fill = "Avg. Weight"
  ) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())
```

The expected pattern: the diagonal should dominate. Because the models are nested (SBA $\subset$ PBA $\subset$ WBA), Occam's razor should produce *asymmetric* off-diagonal errors — the WBA should sometimes be classified as PBA or SBA when its parameters mimic simpler models, but the SBA should rarely be classified as WBA when extra parameters are unnecessary. This asymmetry is a *feature* of the comparison pipeline, not a bug.

### Model Comparison Calibration

A well-calibrated comparison procedure assigns a stacking weight of, say, 0.7 to a model that turns out to be the true generator roughly 70% of the time. We assess this by binning stacking weights and computing the empirical frequency with which the candidate was the true model.

```{r 09_ml_model_calibration}
ml_cal_long <- ml_recovery_df |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("weight_"),
               names_to = "candidate", values_to = "stacking_weight") |>
  mutate(
    candidate = str_remove(candidate, "weight_"),
    is_true   = as.integer(candidate == true_model)
  )

n_bins <- 8
ml_cal_binned <- ml_cal_long |>
  mutate(bin = cut(stacking_weight,
                   breaks = seq(0, 1, length.out = n_bins + 1),
                   include.lowest = TRUE)) |>
  group_by(candidate, bin) |>
  summarise(
    bin_mid    = mean(stacking_weight),
    observed   = mean(is_true),
    N_datasets = n(),
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    SE    = sqrt((observed * (1 - observed)) / pmax(N_datasets, 1)),
    Lower = pmax(0, observed - 1.96 * SE),
    Upper = pmin(1, observed + 1.96 * SE)
  )

ggplot(ml_cal_binned, aes(x = bin_mid, y = observed, colour = candidate)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Lower, ymax = Upper, fill = candidate),
              alpha = 0.15, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Multilevel Model Comparison Calibration",
    subtitle = "Points on the diagonal = well-calibrated stacking weights.\nRibbons = 95% CI; wider ribbons reflect fewer datasets in that bin.",
    x = "Mean Stacking Weight (Binned)",
    y = "Empirical Frequency (True Generating Model)",
    colour = "Candidate", fill = "Candidate"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Points on the diagonal indicate perfect calibration: a stacking weight of 0.7 means the model is the true generator 70% of the time. A curve bowed above the diagonal signals underconfidence (the procedure hedges too much); a curve below signals overconfidence. The multilevel comparison has additional sources of uncertainty relative to the single-agent case — partial pooling, correlation estimation, and funnel geometry — so some additional calibration noise is expected.

## Summary: What the Multilevel Pipeline Adds

The multilevel extension answers the *population-level* question: how do people in general weight evidence, and how much do they vary? By using the reparameterised $(\rho_j, \kappa_j)$ parameterisation — not the raw $(w_d, w_s)$ shown to be unidentifiable in the single-agent case — we preserve the identifiability gains while gaining partial pooling.

The full pipeline mirrors the single-agent workflow:

1. **Model quality:** Diagnostics, trace plots, and summary statistics confirmed clean sampling for all three multilevel models.

2. **Parameter recovery:** Individual $\rho$ values recover well (identified from conflict trials); $\kappa$ is noisier but partially regularised by the hierarchical prior. Partial pooling pulls extreme agents toward the population mean, as expected.

3. **SBC certification:** The multilevel SBC established that the Stan sampler is globally unbiased for the ML-WBA and ML-PBA population parameters — a critical check given the more complex posterior geometry.

4. **Model comparison:** The three-way LOO comparison with pointwise ELPD visualizations showed *where* each model's advantage lies (high-conflict trials for non-balanced populations). Scenario case studies demonstrated the nested Occam's razor effect: simpler populations are correctly attributed to simpler models.

5. **Model recovery:** The confusion matrix and calibration curves quantified the comparison procedure's reliability across many simulated populations, confirming appropriate asymmetric error patterns from the nested model structure.

The key lesson: **the reparameterisation that solved the single-agent identifiability problem is equally essential at the multilevel level.** The hierarchical prior partially constrains the scale, but it does not eliminate the ridge in each agent's conditional likelihood. Only by separating allocation ($\rho$) from scaling ($\kappa$) can we obtain meaningful individual-level parameter estimates — and by extension, meaningful population-level summaries of how people vary.

## Mixed Strategies: When the Population Is Heterogeneous

Every analysis above assumed a *homogeneous* population: all agents were drawn from the same generative model. In practice, this assumption is almost certainly wrong. A real experimental sample plausibly contains people who use qualitatively different strategies — some may integrate evidence at face value (SBA-like), while others systematically overweight social or direct evidence (WBA-like). What happens to our pipeline when the population is a genuine mixture?

This section merges data from two generating models — half the participants behave as Simple Bayesian Agents, half as Weighted Bayesian Agents with a strong social bias — and examines whether the hierarchical models and the comparison pipeline can still produce useful inference.

Because no single hierarchical model can correctly represent a bimodal population distribution, we should *not* expect population-level parameters to be meaningful. The scientific question shifts from "what does the average person do?" to "which strategy does *each* person use?" This motivates a focus on **individual-level parameter recovery** and **per-agent model comparison**, skipping the usual population-level parameter interpretation.

### Generating the Mixed Population
```{r 09_mixed_population_setup}
set.seed(2026)
J_mix <- 40  # 20 SBA + 20 WBA agents

# --- SBA agents: strict Bayesian integration (wd = 1, ws = 1) ---
sba_rhos   <- rep(0.5, 20)
sba_kappas <- rep(2.0, 20)

# --- WBA agents: strong social bias, higher total sensitivity ---
# Draw from a population distribution to add realistic variation
wba_raw <- MASS::mvrnorm(
  20,
  mu    = c(qlogis(0.25), log(2.5)),   # rho ≈ 0.25, kappa ≈ 2.5
  Sigma = matrix(c(0.25, -0.08, -0.08, 0.12), 2)
)
wba_rhos   <- plogis(wba_raw[, 1])
wba_kappas <- exp(wba_raw[, 2])

# Combine and shuffle so agent IDs don't reveal strategy
agent_order   <- sample(J_mix)
all_rhos      <- c(sba_rhos, wba_rhos)[agent_order]
all_kappas    <- c(sba_kappas, wba_kappas)[agent_order]
true_strategy <- c(rep("SBA", 20), rep("WBA", 20))[agent_order]

# Generate trial-level data
mixed_stan_data <- generate_ml_data(all_rhos, all_kappas,
                                     evidence_combinations, n_reps = 2)

# Store ground truth for recovery plots
mixed_truth <- tibble(
  agent_id       = seq_len(J_mix),
  true_rho       = all_rhos,
  true_kappa     = all_kappas,
  true_wd        = all_rhos * all_kappas,
  true_ws        = (1 - all_rhos) * all_kappas,
  true_strategy  = true_strategy
)

cat(sprintf("Mixed population: %d agents (%d SBA + %d WBA)\n",
            J_mix, sum(true_strategy == "SBA"), sum(true_strategy == "WBA")))
cat(sprintf("Total trials: %d | Trials per agent: %d\n",
            mixed_stan_data$N, mixed_stan_data$N / J_mix))
```
```{r 09_mixed_population_viz}
ggplot(mixed_truth, aes(x = true_wd, y = true_ws,
                         colour = true_strategy, shape = true_strategy)) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = agent_id), nudge_x = 0.04, size = 3, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("SBA" = 16, "WBA" = 17)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Mixed Population: True Agent Parameters",
    subtitle = "SBA agents cluster at (1, 1); WBA agents are socially biased with higher total sensitivity",
    x        = expression("True " * w[d]),
    y        = expression("True " * w[s]),
    colour   = "Strategy", shape = "Strategy"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The SBA agents form a tight cluster at $(w_d, w_s) = (1, 1)$ — they are all identical by construction. The WBA agents scatter around $(w_d, w_s) \approx (0.6, 1.9)$, each with their own idiosyncratic weights drawn from the population distribution. No single Gaussian population on the $(\text{logit}(\rho), \log(\kappa))$ scale can represent this bimodal structure.

### Fitting All Three Multilevel Models
```{r 09_mixed_fit, message=FALSE}
if (regenerate_simulations) {

  fit_mix_sba <- mod_ml_sba$sample(
    data = mixed_stan_data, chains = 4, parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.9, refresh = 0
  )

  fit_mix_pba <- mod_ml_pba$sample(
    data = mixed_stan_data, chains = 4, parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.95, refresh = 0
  )

  fit_mix_wba <- mod_ml_wba$sample(
    data = mixed_stan_data, chains = 4, parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup = 1000, iter_sampling = 1000,
    adapt_delta = 0.95, refresh = 0
  )

  # Save each fit individually with $save_object() so draws survive sessions
  fit_mix_sba$save_object("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_sba.rds")
  fit_mix_pba$save_object("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_pba.rds")
  fit_mix_wba$save_object("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_wba.rds")

} else {
  fit_mix_sba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_sba.rds")
  fit_mix_pba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_pba.rds")
  fit_mix_wba <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_fit_mix_wba.rds")
}

cat("=== Mixed Population: Model Diagnostics ===\n")
check_diagnostics(fit_mix_sba, "ML-SBA (mixed)")
check_diagnostics(fit_mix_pba, "ML-PBA (mixed)")
check_diagnostics(fit_mix_wba, "ML-WBA (mixed)")
```

### Why Population Parameters Are Misleading

Before turning to individual-level analysis, let's check what the ML-WBA's population parameters say about this mixed population.
```{r 09_mixed_population_misleading}
draws_mix <- as_draws_df(fit_mix_wba$draws())

# Population-level summaries
pop_mix_summary <- fit_mix_wba$summary(
  variables = c("mu[1]", "mu[2]", "sigma[1]", "sigma[2]", "Omega[1,2]",
                "pop_rho", "pop_kappa")
)

cat("=== ML-WBA Population Parameter Estimates (Mixed Data) ===\n")
pop_mix_summary |>
  select(variable, mean, q5, q95) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Population parameters estimated from mixed-strategy data")

# Extract estimated individual rhos and show the distribution
mix_est_rho <- map_dbl(1:J_mix, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("rho[", .x, "]")]]))

ggplot() +
  geom_histogram(data = tibble(rho = mix_est_rho,
                                strategy = mixed_truth$true_strategy),
                 aes(x = rho, fill = strategy),
                 bins = 20, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = plogis(mean(draws_mix$`mu[1]`))),
             colour = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text",
           x = plogis(mean(draws_mix$`mu[1]`)) + 0.03,
           y = Inf, vjust = 2,
           label = sprintf("Pop. mean ρ = %.2f", plogis(mean(draws_mix$`mu[1]`))),
           colour = "red", size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  labs(
    title    = "The Population Mean Is Meaningless for a Mixture",
    subtitle = "Estimated individual ρ values form two clusters, but the population mean (red dashed) describes nobody",
    x        = expression("Estimated " * rho[j]),
    y        = "Count",
    fill     = "True Strategy"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The histogram tells the story clearly. The estimated $\rho_j$ values form two modes — one near 0.5 (the SBA agents) and one near 0.25 (the WBA agents). But the population mean $\rho$ sits somewhere in between, averaging over two qualitatively different strategies. This "mildly socially biased" summary describes *nobody* in the actual population. **When the population is heterogeneous, population-level parameters from a single hierarchical model can be actively misleading.** This is why individual-level analysis matters.

### Individual Parameter Recovery
```{r 09_mixed_individual_recovery, fig.width=10, fig.height=5}
# Extract individual posteriors
mix_recovery <- mixed_truth |>
  mutate(
    est_rho   = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("rho[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_kappa = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("kappa[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_wd    = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("weight_direct[", .x, "]")]])),
    est_ws    = map_dbl(agent_id, ~ mean(draws_mix[[paste0("weight_social[", .x, "]")]]))
  )

# ρ recovery
p_mix_rho <- ggplot(mix_recovery, aes(x = true_rho, y = est_rho,
                                        colour = true_strategy)) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = expression("Recovery: " * rho * " (relative allocation)"),
       x = expression("True " * rho[j]),
       y = expression("Estimated " * rho[j]),
       colour = "True Strategy")

# κ recovery
p_mix_kappa <- ggplot(mix_recovery, aes(x = true_kappa, y = est_kappa,
                                          colour = true_strategy)) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  labs(title = expression("Recovery: " * kappa * " (total evidence scaling)"),
       x = expression("True " * kappa[j]),
       y = expression("Estimated " * kappa[j]),
       colour = "True Strategy")

p_mix_rho + p_mix_kappa +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Despite the population model being structurally wrong (a unimodal Gaussian fitted to a bimodal mixture), the individual-level parameter recovery is surprisingly good. The partial pooling pulls the two groups slightly toward each other, but the separation is preserved. The $\rho$ estimates cleanly separate SBA agents ($\rho \approx 0.5$) from WBA agents ($\rho \approx 0.25$). The $\kappa$ estimates are noisier — consistent with our earlier finding that total evidence scaling is harder to identify from binary outcomes — but still show the right direction.

This works because the hierarchical prior provides mild regularisation without forcing homogeneity. The estimated population SD ($\sigma$ on the logit/log scales) absorbs the between-group variance, allowing the individual-level posteriors enough room to reflect both strategies. It is not an ideal fit — a formal mixture model (Ch. 8) would be more appropriate — but it is a reasonable one.

### Per-Agent Model Comparison

The population-level LOO comparison tells us which model best predicts *the aggregate dataset*. But the more informative question is: which model best predicts *each individual agent's* behaviour? We decompose the total LOO into agent-level contributions.
```{r 09_mixed_agent_loo}
# Compute trial-level LOO for all three models
loo_mix_sba <- loo(fit_mix_sba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
loo_mix_pba <- loo(fit_mix_pba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
loo_mix_wba <- loo(fit_mix_wba$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))

# Population-level comparison (for reference)
cat("=== Population-Level Comparison (Mixed Data) ===\n")
print(loo_compare(list("ML-SBA" = loo_mix_sba,
                        "ML-PBA" = loo_mix_pba,
                        "ML-WBA" = loo_mix_wba)))

ml_mix_weights <- loo_model_weights(
  list("ML-SBA" = loo_mix_sba, "ML-PBA" = loo_mix_pba, "ML-WBA" = loo_mix_wba),
  method = "stacking"
)
cat("\nStacking weights:\n")
print(round(ml_mix_weights, 3))

# Decompose into per-agent ELPD sums
agent_elpd <- tibble(
  trial        = seq_len(mixed_stan_data$N),
  agent_id     = mixed_stan_data$agent_id,
  elpd_sba     = loo_mix_sba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_pba     = loo_mix_pba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"],
  elpd_wba     = loo_mix_wba$pointwise[, "elpd_loo"]
) |>
  group_by(agent_id) |>
  summarise(
    elpd_SBA = sum(elpd_sba),
    elpd_PBA = sum(elpd_pba),
    elpd_WBA = sum(elpd_wba),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(mixed_truth |> select(agent_id, true_strategy), by = "agent_id") |>
  mutate(
    best_model = case_when(
      elpd_SBA >= elpd_PBA & elpd_SBA >= elpd_WBA ~ "SBA",
      elpd_PBA >= elpd_SBA & elpd_PBA >= elpd_WBA ~ "PBA",
      TRUE ~ "WBA"
    ),
    # ELPD advantage of the best flexible model over the SBA baseline
    elpd_advantage = pmax(elpd_PBA, elpd_WBA) - elpd_SBA
  )
```
```{r 09_mixed_agent_loo_viz, fig.width=10, fig.height=5}
# Left: per-agent ELPD advantage coloured by true strategy
p_agent_advantage <- ggplot(agent_elpd,
       aes(x = reorder(agent_id, elpd_advantage),
           y = elpd_advantage,
           fill = true_strategy)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Per-Agent ELPD: Best Flexible Model vs. SBA",
    subtitle = "Positive = flexible model predicts better. Negative = SBA sufficient.",
    x        = "Agent (sorted by advantage)",
    y        = "ELPD Advantage (max(PBA, WBA) − SBA)",
    fill     = "True Strategy"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.y  = element_text(size = 6),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Right: scatter of per-agent SBA vs WBA ELPD
p_agent_scatter <- ggplot(agent_elpd,
       aes(x = elpd_SBA, y = elpd_WBA,
           colour = true_strategy, shape = best_model)) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("SBA" = 16, "PBA" = 15, "WBA" = 17)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Agent-Level LOO: SBA vs. WBA",
    subtitle = "Points above diagonal: WBA predicts better. Shape = winning model.",
    x        = "Agent ELPD under ML-SBA",
    y        = "Agent ELPD under ML-WBA",
    colour   = "True Strategy", shape = "Best Model"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p_agent_advantage | p_agent_scatter
```

The bar chart on the left reveals a clean separation. The WBA agents (purple) consistently show positive ELPD advantages — the flexible model predicts their behaviour much better than the SBA. The SBA agents (green) hover near zero or show slight negative advantages — the SBA is sufficient, and the flexible models' extra parameters represent wasted complexity. The scatter plot on the right makes the same point geometrically: WBA agents sit above the diagonal (ML-WBA predicts better), while SBA agents sit on or below it.

### Individual-Level Confusion Matrix
```{r 09_mixed_confusion, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
# Build the confusion matrix
mix_confusion <- agent_elpd |>
  count(true_strategy, best_model) |>
  group_by(true_strategy) |>
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    true_strategy = factor(true_strategy, levels = c("SBA", "WBA")),
    best_model    = factor(best_model, levels = c("SBA", "PBA", "WBA"))
  ) |>
  complete(true_strategy, best_model, fill = list(n = 0, proportion = 0))

# Table
mix_confusion |>
  select(-n) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = best_model, values_from = proportion) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
               caption = "Individual-Level Strategy Classification: Mixed Population")

# Heatmap
ggplot(mix_confusion, aes(x = best_model, y = true_strategy, fill = proportion)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%d\n(%s)", n, scales::percent(proportion, accuracy = 1))),
            size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "mako", direction = -1, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev) +
  labs(
    title = "Individual-Level Strategy Recovery: Mixed Population",
    subtitle = "Rows = true generating strategy. Columns = model with highest per-agent ELPD.",
    x     = "Assigned Model (Max Agent ELPD)",
    y     = "True Strategy",
    fill  = "Classification\nRate"
  ) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())
```

### Visualising the Classification Boundary

What separates correctly classified agents from misclassified ones? We plot the agents in the estimated $(\rho, \kappa)$ space, marking classifications.
```{r 09_mixed_classification_boundary}
mix_class_data <- mix_recovery |>
  left_join(agent_elpd |> select(agent_id, best_model, elpd_advantage),
            by = "agent_id") |>
  mutate(correct = (true_strategy == "SBA" & best_model == "SBA") |
                   (true_strategy == "WBA" & best_model %in% c("PBA", "WBA")))

ggplot(mix_class_data,
       aes(x = est_rho, y = est_kappa,
           colour = true_strategy, shape = correct)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 2.0, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("SBA" = "#4daf4a", "WBA" = "#984ea3")) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("TRUE" = 16, "FALSE" = 4),
                     labels = c("TRUE" = "Correct", "FALSE" = "Misclassified")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Classification in Estimated Parameter Space",
    subtitle = "Dotted lines show SBA reference values (ρ = 0.5, κ = 2.0). Crosses = misclassified.",
    x        = expression("Estimated " * rho[j]),
    y        = expression("Estimated " * kappa[j]),
    colour   = "True Strategy", shape = "Classification"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Misclassifications, when they occur, concentrate near the boundary between the two strategy clusters — agents whose estimated $\rho$ sits between 0.35 and 0.5, where the SBA and WBA strategies are hardest to discriminate.

### Discussion: What the Mixed-Population Analysis Reveals

This analysis demonstrates three important lessons for applied cognitive modelling:

**1. Population parameters are misleading for heterogeneous groups.** The ML-WBA's estimated population mean $\rho$ averaged over two qualitatively different strategies to produce a summary that describes nobody. This is not a failure of the hierarchical model *per se* — it is a structural consequence of fitting a unimodal population distribution to a bimodal mixture. When you suspect strategy heterogeneity, do not interpret population means at face value.

**2. Individual-level recovery succeeds despite population-level misspecification.** The ML-WBA, though structurally unable to represent a mixture population, still recovers individual $\rho_j$ and $\kappa_j$ values well enough to separate the two groups. The estimated population SD absorbs the between-group variance, and the partial pooling is mild enough that the bimodal structure in the individual estimates is preserved. A formal mixture model (as in Ch. 8) would be more appropriate — it would estimate the mixing proportion and the within-group population distributions directly — but the standard hierarchical model is a surprisingly robust approximation.

**3. Per-agent model comparison identifies strategy types.** By decomposing the overall LOO into agent-level contributions, we can classify individuals as SBA-type or WBA-type with reasonable accuracy. This connects directly to the mixture modelling approach from Ch. 8: where a mixture model infers strategy membership *within* the generative model, per-agent LOO achieves a similar classification *post hoc* through predictive comparison.

The practical implication for researchers: if you suspect your participants use different strategies, always look beyond population-level summaries. Examine individual parameter estimates for multimodality, and use per-agent model comparison to classify participants before interpreting group effects.

## Beyond Weighted Bayes: Circular Inference

The three models above — SBA, PBA, WBA — share a common structural assumption: each source of evidence is counted exactly once. Simonsen et al. (2021), following Jardri & Denève (2013), found that this assumption is systematically violated — particularly in patients with schizophrenia. Their best-fitting model was *circular inference*: evidence from one source is fed back into the system and counted multiple times.

### Why Pseudocounts Are Not Enough for Circular Inference

The pseudocount framework has no natural way to represent evidence being counted *more than once*. In the WBA, $w_d \cdot k_1$ rescales the evidence — it treats 6 blue marbles as if they were 9, for example — but the integration is still additive and single-pass. Circular inference claims that evidence loops back and amplifies itself, producing a qualitatively different pattern: response curves become *steeper* for the amplified source, not just *shifted*.

The key insight is to work on the **log-odds** (logit) scale:

$$\ell_\text{combined} = w_s \cdot \alpha_\text{self} \cdot (\ell_\text{self} - \ell_0) + w_o \cdot \alpha_\text{social} \cdot (\ell_\text{social} - \ell_0) + \ell_0$$

where $\ell_0 = \log(\alpha_0 / \beta_0)$ is the log-odds prior, $\alpha_\text{self} \geq 1$ and $\alpha_\text{social} \geq 1$ are **loop parameters** controlling how many times each source's evidence is counted. When $\alpha = 1$ and $w = 1$ for both sources, the model reduces to simple Bayes on the log-odds scale.

Note that the likelihood is now **Bernoulli, not BetaBinomial**. This is not arbitrary — it reflects a different cognitive claim. The BetaBinomial was appropriate when the agent's belief was a full probability distribution (the Beta posterior). On the log-odds scale, the belief is a single real number $\ell$; the distributional uncertainty is captured by the posterior over parameters, not by a Beta within each trial.

```{r 09_circular_inference_r}
circularInferenceModel <- function(
    blue1, total1, blue2, total2,
    w_self = 1, w_other = 1,
    alpha_self = 1, alpha_other = 1,
    alpha0 = 0.5, beta0 = 0.5
) {
  lo_self   <- log((alpha0 + blue1)  / (beta0 + total1 - blue1))
  lo_social <- log((alpha0 + blue2)  / (beta0 + total2 - blue2))
  lo_prior  <- log(alpha0 / beta0)

  lo_combined <- w_self  * alpha_self  * (lo_self   - lo_prior) +
                 w_other * alpha_other * (lo_social - lo_prior) +
                 lo_prior

  p_blue <- plogis(lo_combined)
  list(
    lo_self      = lo_self,
    lo_social    = lo_social,
    lo_combined  = lo_combined,
    p_blue       = p_blue,
    choice       = ifelse(p_blue > 0.5, "Blue", "Red")
  )
}

# Visualise: compare WBA (pseudocount) vs. circular inference
ci_comparison <- expand_grid(blue1 = 0:8, blue2 = 0:3) |>
  mutate(
    wba_p  = map2_dbl(blue1, blue2, ~ {
      ap <- 0.5 + 1.0 * .x + 0.5 * .y
      bp <- 0.5 + 1.0 * (8 - .x) + 0.5 * (3 - .y)
      ap / (ap + bp)
    }),
    ci_p   = map2_dbl(blue1, blue2, ~ {
      circularInferenceModel(
        blue1 = .x, total1 = 8,
        blue2 = .y, total2 = 3,
        w_self = 1, w_other = 0.5,
        alpha_self = 3, alpha_other = 1
      )$p_blue
    }),
    social_evidence = factor(
      blue2, levels = 0:3,
      labels = c("Clear Red", "Maybe Red", "Maybe Blue", "Clear Blue")
    )
  )

ci_long <- ci_comparison |>
  pivot_longer(c(wba_p, ci_p), names_to = "model", values_to = "p_blue") |>
  mutate(model = recode(model,
                        "wba_p" = "WBA (w_d=1, w_s=0.5)",
                        "ci_p"  = "Circular (w_d=1, w_s=0.5, α_self=3)"))

ggplot(ci_long, aes(x = blue1, y = p_blue,
                    colour = social_evidence, group = social_evidence)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  facet_wrap(~ model) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "plasma", end = 0.85) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:8) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "WBA vs. Circular Inference: Response Curves",
    subtitle = "Circular inference steepens the self-evidence curves\nwithout uniformly raising them — a qualitatively different signature",
    x        = "Blue marbles in direct sample",
    y        = "P(choose blue)",
    colour   = "Social evidence"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The key visual signature: the WBA curves are roughly parallel — increasing `blue1` shifts the probability by approximately the same amount regardless of the social evidence level. The circular inference curves are steeper for direct evidence (because $\alpha_\text{self} = 3$ amplifies the self-evidence log-odds), producing a pattern where the agent is more *decisive* about strong direct evidence but similarly uncertain about ambiguous evidence. This qualitative difference in curve *shape* is what distinguishes overcounting from mere reweighting, and it is the feature that makes the two models experimentally discriminable.

### Stan Implementation: Circular Inference Agent

```{r 09_stan_circular_inference}
CircularAgent_stan <- "
// Circular Inference Agent (Jardri & Deneve 2013; Jardri et al. 2017).
// Bernoulli likelihood (not BetaBinomial) — see observation model discussion.
data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  array[N] int<lower=0, upper=1> choice;
  array[N] int<lower=0> blue1;
  array[N] int<lower=0> total1;
  array[N] int<lower=0> blue2;
  array[N] int<lower=0> total2;
}

transformed data {
  real alpha0 = 0.5;
  real beta0  = 0.5;
  real lo_prior = log(alpha0 / beta0);
}

parameters {
  real<lower=0> w_self;
  real<lower=0> w_other;
  real<lower=0> alpha_self_m1;    // excess over 1: alpha_self = 1 + alpha_self_m1
  real<lower=0> alpha_other_m1;
}

transformed parameters {
  real<lower=1> alpha_self  = 1.0 + alpha_self_m1;
  real<lower=1> alpha_other = 1.0 + alpha_other_m1;
}

model {
  target += lognormal_lpdf(w_self  | 0, 0.5);
  target += lognormal_lpdf(w_other | 0, 0.5);
  // Half-normal prior on excess loop strength (alpha >= 1 by construction)
  target += normal_lpdf(alpha_self_m1  | 0, 1) - normal_lccdf(0 | 0, 1);
  target += normal_lpdf(alpha_other_m1 | 0, 1) - normal_lccdf(0 | 0, 1);

  for (i in 1:N) {
    real lo_self_i   = log((alpha0 + blue1[i]) /
                           (beta0  + total1[i] - blue1[i]));
    real lo_social_i = log((alpha0 + blue2[i]) /
                           (beta0  + total2[i] - blue2[i]));
    real lo_combined = w_self  * alpha_self  * (lo_self_i   - lo_prior) +
                       w_other * alpha_other * (lo_social_i - lo_prior) +
                       lo_prior;
    target += bernoulli_logit_lpmf(choice[i] | lo_combined);
  }
}

generated quantities {
  vector[N] log_lik;
  array[N] int prior_pred;
  array[N] int posterior_pred;

  // Prior draws for prior predictive checks
  real ws_pr  = lognormal_rng(0, 0.5);
  real wo_pr  = lognormal_rng(0, 0.5);
  real as_pr  = 1.0 + abs(normal_rng(0, 1));
  real ao_pr  = 1.0 + abs(normal_rng(0, 1));

  for (i in 1:N) {
    real lo_self_i   = log((alpha0 + blue1[i]) /
                           (beta0  + total1[i] - blue1[i]));
    real lo_social_i = log((alpha0 + blue2[i]) /
                           (beta0  + total2[i] - blue2[i]));

    real lo_post = w_self  * alpha_self  * (lo_self_i   - lo_prior) +
                   w_other * alpha_other * (lo_social_i - lo_prior) +
                   lo_prior;

    log_lik[i]        = bernoulli_logit_lpmf(choice[i] | lo_post);
    posterior_pred[i] = bernoulli_logit_rng(lo_post);

    real lo_prior_pred = ws_pr * as_pr * (lo_self_i   - lo_prior) +
                         wo_pr * ao_pr * (lo_social_i - lo_prior) +
                         lo_prior;
    prior_pred[i] = bernoulli_logit_rng(lo_prior_pred);
  }
}
"

write_stan_file(CircularAgent_stan,
                dir      = "stan/",
                basename = "ch9_circular_agent.stan")

mod_circular <- cmdstan_model("stan/ch9_circular_agent.stan", dir = "simmodels")
```

The parameterisation of the loop parameters deserves attention. We estimate `alpha_self_m1` $\geq 0$ and reconstruct $\alpha_\text{self} = 1 + \text{alpha\_self\_m1}$. This enforces the constraint $\alpha \geq 1$ (evidence is counted at least once) without placing a hard `<lower=1>` bound on the parameter, which would create boundary geometry pathologies. The half-normal prior on the excess provides gentle regularisation toward $\alpha = 1$ (no overcounting), which is the scientifically conservative default.

### SBC: Circular Inference Agent

The circular inference model has four parameters — more than the PBA or reparameterised WBA — and the loop parameters interact multiplicatively with the weights. SBC is essential to verify that the sampler can identify all four parameters jointly.

```{r 09_sbc_circular, message=FALSE}
if (regenerate_simulations) {

  N_sbc_ci <- nrow(evidence_combinations) * 5

  sbc_generator_ci <- SBC_generator_function(
    function(N) {
      ws    <- rlnorm(1, 0, 0.5)
      wo    <- rlnorm(1, 0, 0.5)
      as_m1 <- abs(rnorm(1, 0, 1))
      ao_m1 <- abs(rnorm(1, 0, 1))
      as_   <- 1 + as_m1
      ao_   <- 1 + ao_m1

      ev <- evidence_combinations |> slice_sample(n = N, replace = TRUE)

      lo_p <- log(0.5 / 0.5)
      lo_s <- log((0.5 + ev$blue1) / (0.5 + ev$total1 - ev$blue1))
      lo_o <- log((0.5 + ev$blue2) / (0.5 + ev$total2 - ev$blue2))
      lo_c <- ws * as_ * (lo_s - lo_p) + wo * ao_ * (lo_o - lo_p) + lo_p
      p    <- plogis(lo_c)
      choices <- rbinom(N, 1, p)

      list(
        variables = list(
          w_self         = ws,
          w_other        = wo,
          alpha_self_m1  = as_m1,
          alpha_other_m1 = ao_m1
        ),
        generated = list(
          N      = N,
          choice = choices,
          blue1  = ev$blue1,
          total1 = ev$total1,
          blue2  = ev$blue2,
          total2 = ev$total2
        )
      )
    },
    N = N_sbc_ci
  )

  sbc_datasets_ci <- generate_datasets(sbc_generator_ci, 500)

  sbc_backend_ci <- SBC::SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample(
    mod_circular,
    chains          = 2,
    iter_warmup     = 500,
    iter_sampling   = 500,
    parallel_chains = 2,
    refresh         = 0,
    show_messages   = FALSE
  )

  sbc_results_ci <- compute_SBC(sbc_datasets_ci, sbc_backend_ci,
                                 keep_fits = FALSE)
  saveRDS(sbc_results_ci, "simmodels/ch9_sbc_ci_results.rds")

} else {
  sbc_results_ci <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_sbc_ci_results.rds")
}

SBC::plot_ecdf_diff(
  sbc_results_ci,
  variables = c("w_self", "w_other", "alpha_self_m1", "alpha_other_m1")
) +
  labs(
    title    = "SBC Calibration: Circular Inference Agent",
    subtitle = "500 datasets. Loop parameters (alpha_*_m1) are harder to\nidentify than weights — expect wider rank intervals."
  )
```

Pay attention to the ECDF-difference plots for the loop parameters. Because `alpha_self_m1` and `alpha_other_m1` multiply the weight parameters, there is a partial tradeoff: a large $w$ with a small $\alpha$ can produce similar log-odds as a small $w$ with a large $\alpha$. If the SBC curves bulge outside the grey band for the loop parameters, this indicates marginal calibration failure — the model may need stronger regularisation or a reparameterisation that separates the multiplicative components more cleanly.

### Fitting and Comparison: WBA vs. Circular Inference

We fit the circular inference model to the Self-Focused scenario data — the same data used for the single-agent WBA — and compare the two models via PSIS-LOO.

```{r 09_fit_circular, message=FALSE}
# --- Fit WBA to Self_Focused for comparison baseline ---
if (regenerate_simulations) {
  fit_wba_self <- mod_weighted$sample(
    data            = stan_data_wba$Self_Focused,
    seed            = 600,
    chains          = 4,
    parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup     = 1000,
    iter_sampling   = 1000,
    refresh         = 0
  )
  fit_wba_self$save_object("simmodels/ch9_fit_wba_self.rds")
} else {
  fit_wba_self <- readRDS("simmodels/ch9_fit_wba_self.rds")
}

# --- Fit Circular Inference to the same data ---
fit_circular_self <- if (regenerate_simulations) {
  fit <- mod_circular$sample(
    data            = stan_data_wba$Self_Focused,
    seed            = 601,
    chains          = 4,
    parallel_chains = 4,
    iter_warmup     = 1000,
    iter_sampling   = 1000,
    refresh         = 0
  )
  fit$save_object("simmodels/ch9_fit_circular_self.rds")
  fit
} else {
  readRDS("simmodels/ch9_fit_circular_self.rds")
}

check_diagnostics(fit_circular_self, "Circular Inference — Self-Focused")

fit_circular_self$summary(
  variables = c("w_self", "w_other", "alpha_self", "alpha_other")
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
               caption = "Circular Inference posteriors: Self-Focused agent")
```

```{r 09_circular_vs_wba_comparison}
loo_wba_self      <- loo(fit_wba_self$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))
loo_circular_self <- loo(fit_circular_self$draws("log_lik", format = "matrix"))

cat("=== Model Comparison: WBA vs. Circular Inference (Self-Focused agent) ===\n")
ci_vs_wba <- loo_compare(
  list(WBA = loo_wba_self, CI = loo_circular_self)
)
print(ci_vs_wba)

diff_ratio <- abs(ci_vs_wba[2, "elpd_diff"] / ci_vs_wba[2, "se_diff"])
cat(sprintf("\n|ELPD diff| / SE = %.2f\n", diff_ratio))

ci_wba_weights <- loo_model_weights(
  list(WBA = loo_wba_self, CI = loo_circular_self),
  method = "stacking"
)
cat("\nBayesian stacking weights:\n")
print(ci_wba_weights)
```

**Expected pattern:** Because the simulated agent was generated by the WBA (pseudocount model with $w_d = 1.5$, $w_s = 0.5$) rather than by circular inference, the WBA should outperform or be competitive with the CI model. The circular inference model has two extra parameters (the loop parameters) that provide no additional explanatory power when the data were generated without overcounting. PSIS-LOO naturally penalises this extra complexity through the effective number of parameters $p_\text{LOO}$.

This is a one-directional test: we verify that the comparison does not spuriously prefer the more complex model. A complete validation would also generate data *from* the circular inference model and verify that the CI model wins in that case. This is left as an exercise — the model recovery pipeline from the previous section provides the template.

### Discussion: When to Use Each Parameterisation

The parameterisations explored in this chapter make different claims about the data-generating process:

**Pseudocount (SBA/PBA/WBA):** The mind treats each source as an independent binomial sample. Evidence is counted once, with optional rescaling. The BetaBinomial observation model claims probability matching — actions sample from the full belief distribution.

**Circular inference (Jardri & Denève 2013):** Evidence is not merely weighted but potentially amplified beyond its face value. The Bernoulli observation model collapses the belief to a single log-odds value; distributional uncertainty is captured by the posterior over parameters. The key predictive signature — steeper response curves for the amplified source — cannot be reproduced by pseudocount weighting alone.

The choice between models should be driven by the scientific question and validated by LOO comparison. For a course task where the goal is to understand differential *trust*, the WBA is appropriate. For a clinical research question about *whether patients overcount direct experience at a mechanistic level*, circular inference is the scientifically motivated model.

## What the Bayesian Workflow Adds

This chapter has done more than fit cognitive models — it has *validated* them through a disciplined pipeline. Prior predictive checks established that the priors of all three models are cognitively plausible. SBC certified that the Stan samplers for the WBA and PBA are unbiased. Single-fit recovery confirmed identifiability. LOO-PIT and sensitivity analysis characterised the limits of the fitted posteriors. The three-way PSIS-LOO comparison arbitrated between competing cognitive hypotheses in a way that penalises complexity and estimates genuine predictive performance.

This pipeline is not bureaucracy. Without SBC, a subtle Stan implementation bug could produce attractive posteriors that are systematically biased. Without LOO-PIT, we cannot distinguish a model that fits from a model that truly predicts. Without the three-way comparison, we cannot know whether the WBA's additional parameter reflects a real cognitive structure or mere overfitting. And without parameter recovery, we would never have discovered that the raw $(w_d, w_s)$ parameterisation was structurally unidentifiable — a problem invisible to standard MCMC diagnostics.

### Limitations of the Current Models

1. **The observation model is itself a hypothesis.** The BetaBinomial claims participants probability-match — sampling their action from the full posterior over $\theta$. If participants are more deterministic (choosing blue whenever $\text{E}[\theta] > 0.5$), the BetaBinomial will overestimate response variability. A softmax observation model with an inverse-temperature parameter would test this alternative; the LOO-PIT hump pattern is the diagnostic signature to watch for.

2. **Trial independence in non-sequential models.** The SBA, PBA, and WBA assume each trial involves a fresh, independent jar. Applying these models to paradigms with across-trial structure requires the sequential extension — with LFO-CV replacing standard LOO.

3. **Fixed prior pseudo-count.** We fix $\alpha_0 = \beta_0 = 0.5$ (Jeffreys prior), assuming no prior knowledge about the jar. A hierarchical extension would estimate a population distribution over $(\alpha_0, \beta_0)$, allowing for individual differences in baseline expectations.

4. **The PBA's identifiability boundary.** When $p \to 0$ or $p \to 1$, one evidence source is entirely ignored. The Beta(2,2) prior keeps the sampler away from these boundaries, but check tail ESS whenever the PBA is fitted to strongly asymmetric agents.

5. **Social evidence encoding and pool-size asymmetry.** Social evidence is encoded as `total2 = 3` pseudo-marbles, making direct and social evidence structurally asymmetric. Recovered weights should be interpreted relative to the encoding, not as absolute measures of social reliance.

### Connections and Extensions

- **Rational inattention models** relax the assumption that all evidence is perfectly observed — the agent may allocate limited attentional resources to each source.
- **Resource-rational models** (Griffiths et al. 2015) ask: what integration strategy is optimal given limited computational resources? The WBA can be interpreted as an approximation to full Bayesian inference under resource constraints.
- **Active inference** (Friston 2010) frames perception and action as a unified process of minimising variational free energy, offering a different theoretical foundation for the same empirical phenomena.

**This is the end of the core modelling sequence.** Chapters 1–9 have
traced the complete Bayesian cognitive modelling workflow: from pizza-stone
physics (Ch. 1), through verbal and formal models of strategic behaviour
(Chs. 2–3), parameter estimation (Ch. 4), model validation (Ch. 5),
hierarchical extensions (Ch. 6), model comparison (Ch. 7), mixture
structures (Ch. 8), and Bayesian cognitive theory (Ch. 9). The tools and
habits of mind established here — generative modelling, principled
validation, honest uncertainty — apply directly to any new domain or model
you encounter.

## Further Reading

- Ma, W. J., Kording, K. P., & Goldreich, D. (2023). *Bayesian models of perception and action: An introduction.* MIT Press.
- Griffiths, T. L., Chater, N., & Tenenbaum, J. B. (Eds.). (2024). *Bayesian models of cognition: Reverse engineering the mind.* MIT Press.
- Goodman, N. D., Tenenbaum, J. B., & The ProbMods Contributors (2016). *Probabilistic Models of Cognition* (2nd ed.). https://probmods.org/
- Bürkner, P.-C., Gabry, J., & Vehtari, A. (2020). Approximate leave-future-out cross-validation for Bayesian time series models. *Journal of Statistical Computation and Simulation*, 90(14), 2499–2523.
- Säilynoja, T., Bürkner, P.-C., & Vehtari, A. (2022). Graphical test for discrete uniformity and its applications in goodness-of-fit evaluation and multiple sample comparison. *Statistics and Computing*, 32, 32.
- Gabry, J., Simpson, D., Vehtari, A., Betancourt, M., & Gelman, A. (2019). Visualization in Bayesian workflow. *Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series A*, 182(2), 389–402.
- Simonsen, A., et al. (2021). Computationally defined markers of uncertainty in the theory of mind predict the severity of clinical symptoms in schizophrenia. *Schizophrenia Bulletin*.
- Jardri, R., & Denève, S. (2013). Circular inferences in schizophrenia. *Brain*, 136(11), 3227–3241.
